verses.text: God sends Jeremiah to the temple court to speak 'all the words,' withholding nothing; perhaps they will hear and repent. Faithfulness keeps back nothing. The preacher must deliver the whole message, hoping for repentance.

God sends Jeremiah to the temple court to speak 'all the words,' withholding nothing; perhaps they will hear and repent. Faithfulness keeps back nothing. The preacher must deliver the whole message, hoping for repentance.

God sends Jeremiah to the temple court to speak 'all the words,' withholding nothing; perhaps they will hear and repent. Faithfulness keeps back nothing. The preacher must deliver the whole message, hoping for repentance.
If they will not hearken, God will make this house 'like Shiloh,' a ruin and a curse. The temple is no charm. Even God's house will be destroyed if His people forsake Him.
If they will not hearken, God will make this house 'like Shiloh,' a ruin and a curse. The temple is no charm. Even God's house will be destroyed if His people forsake Him.
If they will not hearken, God will make this house 'like Shiloh,' a ruin and a curse. The temple is no charm. Even God's house will be destroyed if His people forsake Him.
The priests and prophets seize Jeremiah, crying, 'Thou shalt surely die,' enraged at his word. Truth provoked them. Those most offended by God's word are often its appointed guardians gone astray.
The priests and prophets seize Jeremiah, crying, 'Thou shalt surely die,' enraged at his word. Truth provoked them. Those most offended by God's word are often its appointed guardians gone astray.
The priests and prophets seize Jeremiah, crying, 'Thou shalt surely die,' enraged at his word. Truth provoked them. Those most offended by God's word are often its appointed guardians gone astray.
Brought before the princes, Jeremiah answers that the LORD sent him and bids them amend their ways. He does not retract. The faithful witness stands by his message before the court.

Brought before the princes, Jeremiah answers that the LORD sent him and bids them amend their ways. He does not retract. The faithful witness stands by his message before the court.

He warns that to kill him is to 'bring innocent blood' on themselves, yet leaves himself in their hands. He commits his life to God. The servant fears God more than men, and trusts Him with the outcome.

He warns that to kill him is to 'bring innocent blood' on themselves, yet leaves himself in their hands. He commits his life to God. The servant fears God more than men, and trusts Him with the outcome.

The princes and people judge him not worthy of death, recalling that Micah prophesied likewise and Hezekiah did not kill him. Precedent defends him. The memory of past faithfulness can shield God's servant.

Urijah, who prophesied similarly, was slain by Jehoiakim, but Ahikam protected Jeremiah. God spares one and not another. The faithful are kept until their work is done, by means God appoints.
